title: The Science Behind the Seven
paragraphs: Before I take you through the house, let me take you through the research. Because these seven personas aren't something I made up over coffee. They're grounded in decades of peer-reviewed psychology, and every one of them maps onto patterns that researchers have documented, measured, and replicated. I've synthesised them into pastoral language because that's where I live. But the scaffolding underneath is clinical.

Let's start with the big one. Dr. John Gottman spent over forty years studying couples at the University of Washington in what became known as the "Love Lab." His research identified four communication patterns that predict the breakdown of a relationship with over 90% accuracy: criticism, contempt, defensiveness, and stonewalling. He called them the Four Horsemen of the Apocalypse. Of those four, stonewalling — the complete shutdown during conflict — is the most misunderstood. Gottman's data showed that approximately 85% of stonewallers are men. Not because men don't care. Because men's physiology floods faster. Their heart rates spike above 100 beats per minute. Stress hormones hit the bloodstream. And when the nervous system hits overload, the brain does the only thing it knows how to do: shut the power off. That's the Wall. The Wall isn't punishing you. The Wall is drowning.

Then there's deflection and minimisation. Attachment theory — built on the pioneering work of John Bowlby and Mary Ainsworth and later applied to adult relationships by Hazan and Shaver — shows us that people who grew up with dismissive or unresponsive caregivers develop what psychologists call a deactivating strategy. They learned young that expressing emotional needs was met with rejection or indifference. So in adulthood, when you try to bring them a problem, they dodge. They redirect. They tell you it's not a big deal. That's not stubbornness. That's a childhood survival mechanism running on autopilot in an adult relationship. That's the Deflector. That's the Minimiser.

The Prosecutor maps directly onto a pattern called DARVO: Deny, Attack, Reverse Victim and Offender. It was first identified by psychologist Jennifer Freyd in 1997 and has since been validated across multiple empirical studies. Research by Harsey, Zurbriggen, and Freyd found that a significant majority of people confronted about wrongdoing deploy this reversal strategy. And here's what should alarm you: experimental studies showed that when bystanders witness DARVO in action, they judge the perpetrator as less responsible and the victim as less believable. The pattern works. That's why people use it. But the same research showed that even brief education about the DARVO pattern significantly reduces its effectiveness. Knowing the name of the trick changes how you respond to it.

Emotional flooding — the Volcano — is what Gottman documented when heart rates cross the threshold during conflict. At that point, the regions of the brain responsible for empathy, problem-solving, and even hearing what the other person is saying go offline. The amygdala hijacks the system. The Volcano isn't choosing to erupt. Their nervous system has been commandeered.

The Performer connects to what therapists identify as codependent conflict avoidance — people-pleasing disguised as repentance. They've learned that offering an immediate, beautiful apology is the fastest way to end the discomfort of being confronted. The tears are real. The words sound right. But the apology is disconnected from any concrete plan for change. It's emotional currency without behavioural follow-through. Research consistently links this pattern to chronic emotional suppression, which doesn't resolve conflict. It buries it. And buried things don't die. They grow roots.

And the Ghost. Gottman's research on "bids for connection" is haunting. In his studies, couples who stayed married responded to their partner's emotional bids — the little moments of reaching out, the glances, the questions, the touches — 86% of the time. Couples who divorced? Thirty-three percent. The Ghost is the person who has gradually, silently, imperceptibly stopped turning toward you. Their body is still in the room. Their heart left months ago. And by the time you notice, they're halfway gone.

Every one of these seven personas is a survival strategy. Every one developed for a reason. Your job isn't to diagnose them. Your job is to recognise the pattern, adjust your approach, and earn your way into a real conversation.
